criteria: The agent has read Section 3.1 completely to understand the sparse attention problem formulation, including the definitions of sparse attention output (Equation 1) and the error bound derivation (Equation 2).

The agent has read Section 3.2 completely to understand why top-k sparse attention fails (budget dynamism due to diverse attention distributions) and how top-p sparse attention (Definition 3.3, Equation 4) addresses this.
The agent has read Section 4.1 completely to understand the Select-then-Prune two-step hierarchical pruning architecture and how Twilight wraps any base sparse attention algorithm.
The agent has read Section 4.2 completely to understand the efficient SpGEMV kernel with INT4 K cache quantization and the binary search top-p kernel.
The agent has read Algorithm 1 to understand the parallel-friendly binary search implementation for top-p token selection.
The agent has read Table 2 to understand the Longbench accuracy results comparing Twilight against Quest, DS, MagicPIG, and Full attention baselines.
The agent has read Table 3 to understand the RULER benchmark accuracy results across sequence lengths 16k, 32k, 64k, and 96k.
The agent has read Table 4 to understand the medium-context accuracy results (GSM8K, COQA, PG-19 perplexity) comparing Twilight to Quest, DS, and Full attention.
The agent has read Appendix B.2 to understand the Group Query Attention (GQA) compatibility design: group-wise token union and varlen attention with flattened paged KV cache.
The agent has read Figure 9 to understand the accuracy-efficiency tradeoff analysis of the threshold p and the chosen operating point (p=0.85 for Longchat, p=0.95 for LLaMA-2/3).
The plan includes implementing the sparse attention computation as described in Equation 1: ô = softmax(q·K^T/√d) Λ_I V, with the index mask Λ_I selecting only the critical tokens.
The plan includes implementing oracle top-p sparse attention (Equation 4): selecting the minimum set of indices I such that the sum of normalized attention weights meets the threshold p.

The plan includes implementing the top-p binary search algorithm (Algorithm 1) using parallel GPU-friendly operations (max, where, sum fused into a single loop) without materializing intermediate variables.
The plan includes the two-step Select-then-Prune architecture: a Token Selector using a conservative large budget followed by a Twilight Pruner that retains only top-p tokens.
The plan includes implementing SpGEMV with INT4 asymmetrically quantized K cache, including per-head dynamic quantization (scale and zero point) in paged memory layout.
The plan includes integrating Quest as the Token Selector base algorithm, with a conservative budget (e.g., 1/4 sparsity), followed by Twilight's top-p pruning.
The plan includes integrating Double Sparsity (DS) as the Token Selector base algorithm with its optimized per-model configurations.
The plan includes supporting Group Query Attention (GQA) by operating at query group granularity, unioning tokens selected by all query heads in a group.
The plan includes accuracy experiments on Longbench (12 tasks) with Longchat-7B-v1.5-32k and LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct, varying the budget from 256 to 8192 for top-k baselines.
The plan includes accuracy experiments on RULER with LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct at context lengths 16k, 32k, 64k, and 96k.
The plan includes medium-context accuracy experiments on GSM8K, COQA, and PG-19 perplexity using LLaMA-2-7B-Chat and LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct, with baselines at budget=128.
The plan includes efficiency experiments benchmarking self-attention speedup on a single A100 GPU at different batch sizes and sequence lengths (10k-30k tokens).
The plan includes ablation study on threshold p by measuring PG-19 perplexity and sparse attention kernel latency across different p values.
The code for sparse attention output (Equation 1) has been implemented: ô = softmax(q·K^T/√d) Λ_I V, applying the index mask Λ_I to select only the critical tokens before computing attention.

The code for the error bound (Equation 2) has been implemented or used to justify the top-p selection objective: minimizing 1 - Σ_{i∈I} W[i] by selecting a subset that maximizes the sum of normalized attention weights.
The code for oracle top-k sparse attention (Equation 3) has been implemented: selecting the top-B indices maximizing the sum of normalized attention weights W[i] as a theoretical upper bound.
The code for oracle top-p sparse attention (Equation 4) has been implemented: selecting the minimum set of indices I such that Σ_{i∈I} W[i] ≥ p, where W are the normalized attention weights.

The top-p binary search algorithm (Algorithm 1) has been implemented on GPU with binary search over threshold values l and r, using element-wise operations (max, where, sum) fuseable into a single loop.
The binary search in Algorithm 1 terminates using the stopping criterion: max(W_2) - min(W_1) < epsilon, where W_1 and W_2 mask elements outside the current search range.

The Select-then-Prune architecture has been implemented: the Token Selector first selects a conservative set of critical tokens I_0, then the Twilight Pruner further prunes to a smaller set I_1 via top-p thresholding.

The Token Selector is implemented as a black-box wrapper that accepts any base sparse attention algorithm (e.g., Quest, DS) and a conservative budget (e.g., 1/4 sparsity) to produce candidate indices I_0.

The Twilight Pruner has been implemented to take the candidate indices I_0 from the Token Selector and produce the final pruned indices I_1 by applying the top-p threshold to the normalized attention weights.

The SpGEMV kernel has been implemented to compute the product of FP16 query vector q and INT4 quantized key matrix K (qFP16 · K_INT4) with paged indexing, adapted from FlashInfer's attention decoding kernel.

The SpGEMV kernel uses a two-stage pipeline: asynchronously loading and dequantizing INT4 K cache from global memory to shared memory (using cp.async) while overlapping with dot product computation of the current block.
The dequantized K cache is stored in FP16 (not FP32) in shared memory to balance accuracy and computation efficiency as a score estimator.
Per-head dynamic INT4 KV quantization has been implemented using per-head scale and zero point stored in FP16, with the same paged memory layout as the K cache, following the QServe design.
The INT4 bit-packing has been implemented: two 4-bit elements are packed into each uint8_t byte in an interleaved manner, with a +128 offset applied to convert INT4 to unsigned values before packing.
Address calculation for the INT4 packed buffer is remapped to stride at 4-bit granularity (effective byte offset halved) to align with the interleaved packing scheme.
The dequantization routine uses fast INT4-to-FP16 type conversion leveraging custom PTX assembly instructions as implemented in NVIDIA FasterTransformer.
The separate INT4 asymmetrically quantized K cache is maintained on GPU alongside the original FP16 KV cache, introducing approximately 1/8 extra KV cache memory overhead.
Load balancing for head-wise dynamic budgets has been implemented by reusing FlashInfer's load balancing algorithm and flattening the head dimension to handle variable token budgets per head.
The Sparse Attention Kernel has been implemented to accept the pruned indices I_1 and compute the final sparse attention output only on the top-p selected tokens.
The first two layers of the model are excluded from sparse attention (using full attention), consistent with the baselines, to ensure fair comparison.
Quest is integrated as the Token Selector base algorithm: Quest's page-level (16 tokens per page) max pooling token selection is used to produce the conservative candidate set I_0.
Double Sparsity (DS) is integrated as the Token Selector base algorithm, using the optimized configurations tuned for each model provided by DS's official repository.
FlashInfer is integrated as the Token Selector base algorithm (trivial full-token selector) to create the FlashInfer-Twi variant.

Group Query Attention (GQA) support has been implemented: for each query group, the union of tokens selected by all query heads in the group is taken, transitioning head-wise dynamism to group-wise dynamism.
The varlen attention kernel with flattened paged KV cache has been implemented: head-wise varlen attention for MHA models and group-wise varlen attention for GQA models (e.g., LLaMA-3.1).
Twilight kernels are implemented in both CUDA and OpenAI Triton as described in Section 4.2.
The hyperparameter p is set to 0.95 for LLaMA-2-7B-Chat and LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct, and 0.85 for Longchat-7B-v1.5-32k.
The Quest kernels have been modified to support batch inference (as the original Quest only supports single-batch inference).
The accuracy evaluation code loads the three models: Longchat-7B-v1.5-32k, LLaMA-2-7B-Chat, and LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct, covering both MHA and GQA attention implementations.

The code for loading and evaluating on all 12 Longbench task types has been implemented: Single-Doc. QA (Qasper, MF-en), Multi-Doc. QA (HotpotQA, 2WikiMQA, Musique), Summarization (GovReport, QMSum, MultiNews), Few-shot (TriviaQA), Synthetic (PR-en), Code (LCC, Repobench-P).
The code for evaluating on RULER with context lengths 16k, 32k, 64k, and 96k (including CWE/FWE for non-retrieval accuracy) has been implemented.
The medium-context evaluation code uses the lm-harness framework for zero-shot GSM8K (flexible/strict) and COQA (em/f1), and computes PG-19 perplexity, with all baselines using a budget of 128.
The efficiency benchmarking code measures self-attention operator latency at different batch sizes and sequence lengths (10k to 30k tokens from Qasper, GovReport, and LCC tasks of Longbench).
The token dropping baseline comparison code has been implemented to evaluate StreamingLLM and SnapKV (budget=4096) against DS-Twilight on Longbench with Longchat-7B-v1.5-32k.
The offloading scenario evaluation code has been implemented to measure latency when the KV cache is stored in CPU memory, comparing Quest vs. Quest-Twi at context lengths 10k, 20k, and 30k.
The code for accuracy evaluation on Longbench (12 tasks) with Quest-Twi, DS-Twi, and Full-Twi on Longchat-7B-v1.5-32k and LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct has been executed successfully without errors.
The code for RULER evaluation with LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct comparing Quest, Quest-Twi, DS, DS-Twi, Full, Full-Twi, and MagicPIG has been executed successfully without errors.
The code for medium-context evaluation (GSM8K, COQA, PG-19 perplexity) with LLaMA-2-7B-Chat and LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct for Quest, DS, Full, and Twilight has been executed successfully without errors.

The self-attention operator speedup benchmarks on a single A100 GPU, comparing SDPA (FA2/MEA), FlashInfer, Quest, FlashInfer-Twi, and Quest-Twi at different batch sizes and sequence lengths, have been executed successfully without errors.
The end-to-end decoding speedup experiments with batch sizes 32 to 256 comparing FlashInfer, Quest, and Quest-Twi (measuring Time-Per-Output-Token) have been executed successfully without errors.

The ablation study on threshold p (measuring PG-19 perplexity and sparse attention kernel latency across p values from 0.65 to 1.0 using Longchat-7B-v1.5-32k) has been executed successfully without errors.
The time breakdown experiment (Token Selector, SpGEMV, Top-p, Sparse Attention) with Quest-Twi on a 32k retrieval task at batch sizes 16, 32, 64 has been executed successfully without errors.

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 2: Twilight with DS (DS-Twi) achieves a +5.7% score improvement over DS on Longchat-7B-v1.5-32k while pruning up to 98% of the tokens selected by the base algorithm.
The execution outcomes match the results in Table 2: Twilight with Quest (Quest-Twi) achieves a +2.5% score improvement over the best Quest budget on Longchat-7B-v1.5-32k.
The execution outcomes match the results in Table 2: Twilight on LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct achieves nearly zero accuracy loss (<1%) with a slight increase in budget usage compared to the base algorithms.
The execution outcomes match the results in Table 3: DS-Twi achieves the highest average RULER score (87.88) across all context lengths, surpassing the non-top-k method MagicPIG.

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 3: Quest-Twi achieves performance comparable to MagicPIG on RULER (Quest-Twi avg. 86.65 vs. MagicPIG best avg. 87.06) despite Quest alone performing significantly worse (77.75 at 4%).

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 4: Twilight achieves near-zero accuracy loss on medium-context tasks (GSM8K, COQA, PG-19) compared to full attention, significantly outperforming Quest and with comparable or better performance than DS.
The execution outcomes match the results in Table 4: the average Twilight budget on medium-context tasks is approximately 90-115 tokens for LLaMA-2-7B-Chat and LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct.

The execution outcomes match the results in Figure 7: Quest-Twi achieves up to 15.8× speedup over FlashAttention2 and 1.4× speedup over Quest for the self-attention operator; FlashInfer-Twi achieves up to 6.5× over FlashAttention2 and 2.4× over FlashInfer.
The execution outcomes match the results in Figure 8: Quest-Twi achieves up to 3.9× speedup in end-to-end TPOT compared to FlashInfer and a 1.35× speedup compared to Quest without Twilight.

The execution outcomes match the results in Figure 9: accuracy and efficiency balance at approximately p=0.85 for Longchat, with p values above 0.90 causing a sharp increase in attention time while p values below 0.85 degrade perplexity.

The execution outcomes match the results in Figure 10: the time breakdown shows Sparse Attention dominates at small batch sizes, while at batch size 64 Quest-Twi outperforms Quest by ~2×, with SpGEMV being the primary overhead in the Pruner.

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 6 (Appendix D): DS-Twilight (avg. 38.71) outperforms StreamingLLM (avg. 32.69) and SnapKV (avg. 36.22) on all 12 Longbench tasks with Longchat-7B-v1.5-32k.

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 7 (Appendix E): Quest-Twi achieves up to 16× speedup over Quest in CPU-offloading scenarios, with Quest-Twi latency remaining nearly constant (~416-528μs) as context length grows from 10k to 30k.

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 5 (Appendix C): the full per-task Longbench scores for all methods and budget settings on both Longchat-7B-v1.5-32k and LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct are reproduced within acceptable margins.
